speaker: ACHILLES
text: I’ll heat his blood with Greekish wine tonight,

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Which with my scimitar I’ll cool tomorrow.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Patroclus, let us feast him to the height.

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Here comes Thersites.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: How now, thou core of envy?

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Thou crusty botch of nature, what’s the news?

speaker: THERSITES
text: Why, thou picture of what thou seemest and

speaker: THERSITES
text: idol of idiot-worshippers, here’s a letter for thee.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: From whence, fragment?

speaker: THERSITES
text: Why, thou full dish of fool, from Troy.

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Who keeps the tent now?

speaker: THERSITES
text: The surgeon’s box or the patient’s wound.

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Well said, adversity. And what need these

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: tricks?

speaker: THERSITES
text: Prithee, be silent, boy. I profit not by thy

speaker: THERSITES
text: talk. Thou art said to be Achilles’ male varlet.

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: “Male varlet,” you rogue! What’s that?

speaker: THERSITES
text: Why, his masculine whore. Now the rotten

speaker: THERSITES
text: diseases of the south, the guts-griping, ruptures,

speaker: THERSITES
text: catarrhs, loads o’ gravel in the back, lethargies,

speaker: THERSITES
text: cold palsies, raw eyes, dirt-rotten livers, whissing

speaker: THERSITES
text: lungs, bladders full of impostume, sciaticas,

speaker: THERSITES
text: limekilns i’ th’ palm, incurable bone-ache, and the

speaker: THERSITES
text: rivelled fee-simple of the tetter, take and take

speaker: THERSITES
text: again such preposterous discoveries.

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Why, thou damnable box of envy, thou,

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: what means thou to curse thus?

speaker: THERSITES
text: Do I curse thee?

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Why, no, you ruinous butt, you whoreson

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: indistinguishable cur, no.

speaker: THERSITES
text: No? Why art thou then exasperate, thou idle

speaker: THERSITES
text: immaterial skein of sleave-silk, thou green sarsenet

speaker: THERSITES
text: flap for a sore eye, thou tassel of a prodigal’s purse,

speaker: THERSITES
text: thou? Ah, how the poor world is pestered with such

speaker: THERSITES
text: waterflies, diminutives of nature!

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Out, gall!

speaker: THERSITES
text: Finch egg!

speaker: ACHILLES
text: My sweet Patroclus, I am thwarted quite

speaker: ACHILLES
text: From my great purpose in tomorrow’s battle.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Here is a letter from Queen Hecuba,

speaker: ACHILLES
text: A token from her daughter, my fair love,

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Both taxing me and gaging me to keep

speaker: ACHILLES
text: An oath that I have sworn. I will not break it.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Fall, Greeks; fail, fame; honor, or go or stay;

speaker: ACHILLES
text: My major vow lies here; this I’ll obey.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Come, come, Thersites, help to trim my tent.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: This night in banqueting must all be spent.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Away, Patroclus.

speaker: THERSITES
text: With too much blood and too little brain,

speaker: THERSITES
text: these two may run mad; but if with too much brain

speaker: THERSITES
text: and too little blood they do, I’ll be a curer of madmen.

speaker: THERSITES
text: Here’s Agamemnon, an honest fellow enough

speaker: THERSITES
text: and one that loves quails, but he has not so much

speaker: THERSITES
text: brain as earwax. And the goodly transformation

speaker: THERSITES
text: of Jupiter there, his brother, the bull—the primitive

speaker: THERSITES
text: statue and oblique memorial of cuckolds, a

speaker: THERSITES
text: thrifty shoeing-horn in a chain, hanging at his

speaker: THERSITES
text: brother’s leg—to what form but that he is should

speaker: THERSITES
text: wit larded with malice and malice forced with

speaker: THERSITES
text: wit turn him to? To an ass were nothing; he is both

speaker: THERSITES
text: ass and ox. To an ox were nothing; he is both ox

speaker: THERSITES
text: and ass. To be a dog, a mule, a cat, a fitchew, a

speaker: THERSITES
text: toad, a lizard, an owl, a puttock, or a herring without

speaker: THERSITES
text: a roe, I would not care; but to be Menelaus! I

speaker: THERSITES
text: would conspire against destiny. Ask me not what I

speaker: THERSITES
text: would be, if I were not Thersites, for I care not to be

speaker: THERSITES
text: the louse of a lazar so I were not Menelaus.

speaker: THERSITES
text: Heyday! Sprites and fires!

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: We go wrong, we go wrong.

speaker: AJAX
text: No, yonder—’tis there, where we see the lights.

speaker: HECTOR
text: I trouble you.

speaker: AJAX
text: No, not a whit.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Here comes himself to guide you.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Welcome, brave Hector. Welcome, princes all.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: So now, fair prince of Troy, I bid good night.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Ajax commands the guard to tend on you.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Thanks, and good night to the Greeks’ general.

speaker: MENELAUS
text: Good night, my lord.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Good night, sweet lord Menelaus.

speaker: THERSITES
text: Sweet draught. “Sweet,” quoth he?

speaker: THERSITES
text: Sweet sink, sweet sewer.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Good night and welcome, both at once, to those

speaker: ACHILLES
text: That go or tarry.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Good night.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Old Nestor tarries, and you too, Diomed.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Keep Hector company an hour or two.

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: I cannot, lord. I have important business,

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: The tide whereof is now.—Good night, great Hector.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Give me your hand.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Follow his torch; he goes to Calchas’ tent.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: I’ll keep you company.

speaker: TROILUS
text: Sweet sir, you honor me.

speaker: HECTOR
text: And so, good night.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Come, come, enter my tent.

speaker: THERSITES
text: That same Diomed’s a false-hearted rogue,

speaker: THERSITES
text: a most unjust knave. I will no more trust him when

speaker: THERSITES
text: he leers than I will a serpent when he hisses. He

speaker: THERSITES
text: will spend his mouth and promise like Brabbler

speaker: THERSITES
text: the hound, but when he performs, astronomers

speaker: THERSITES
text: foretell it; it is prodigious, there will come some

speaker: THERSITES
text: change. The sun borrows of the moon when

speaker: THERSITES
text: Diomed keeps his word. I will rather leave to see

speaker: THERSITES
text: Hector than not to dog him. They say he keeps a

speaker: THERSITES
text: Trojan drab and uses the traitor Calchas his tent.

speaker: THERSITES
text: I’ll after. Nothing but lechery! All incontinent varlets!
